nodes.label: Agent Clusters
nodes.short_description: Systems that run many agents together as a managed group or fleet.

nodes.label: Agent Collaboration
nodes.short_description: Protocols, patterns and platforms for agents to collaborate on shared tasks.

nodes.label: Agent to Agent
nodes.short_description: Communication protocols and transports that let agents talk directly to other agents.

nodes.label: Experiments
nodes.short_description: Experimental agent-to-agent communication projects that don't fit established transport or protocol categories.
nodes.long_description: Research-grade and exploratory work on how agents might talk to each other — novel channels, encodings, or interaction modes. Sits alongside formal A2A protocols as a sandbox for ideas that haven't hardened into standards.

nodes.label: Voices
nodes.short_description: Audio-channel experiments where agents communicate via sound rather than text or structured messages.
nodes.long_description: Projects exploring voice or acoustic signaling as an inter-agent transport — agents emitting tones, speech, or compressed audio protocols to exchange information. A narrow experimental slot within agent-to-agent research.
nodes.examples_narrative: Gibberlink is the canonical example, where two voice agents detect each other and switch to a machine-efficient acoustic protocol.

nodes.label: Agent Tools
nodes.short_description: Tools and services that agents invoke as capabilities, rather than frameworks for building agents themselves.
nodes.long_description: Top-level segment for utilities designed to be called by agents — communications, side-effect actions, and similar capability providers. Distinct from runtimes, frameworks, or harnesses; these are the things at the other end of a tool call.

nodes.label: Comms
nodes.short_description: Messaging and communication tools that agents use to send, receive, or coordinate via human-style channels.
nodes.long_description: Services exposing email, chat, or mailbox-like primitives to agents as callable tools. Typically shipped as MCP servers or SDKs so an agent can send messages, read inboxes, and coordinate over standard comms channels.
nodes.examples_narrative: MCP Agent Mail illustrates the slot by giving agents a mailbox-style interface over MCP.

nodes.label: Agents
nodes.short_description: General agent projects and agent subtypes that do not fit more specific segments.

nodes.label: Autonomous
nodes.short_description: Long-running autonomous agents that pursue goals with minimal human supervision.
nodes.long_description: Agents designed to operate open-endedly against a goal, planning and executing over extended horizons without turn-by-turn prompting. Distinct from chat assistants, harnesses, or task-specific agents — the defining trait is self-directed operation.

nodes.label: Trading
nodes.short_description: Autonomous agents that execute trading strategies in financial or crypto markets.
nodes.long_description: Self-directed agents that monitor markets, form views, and place trades without per-decision human approval. Typically shipped as runnable services or bots wired into exchange APIs.
nodes.examples_narrative: AI-Trader is an example of a goal-driven trading agent operating autonomously against market data.

nodes.label: Autonomous Agent Creation
nodes.short_description: Agents (or platforms) whose purpose is to spawn and configure other autonomous agents.
nodes.long_description: Meta-level tools where the output is a new agent — skill bundles, agent factories, or systems that compose runnable agents from specs. Sits above individual agents in the stack as a creation layer.
nodes.examples_narrative: BankrBot Skills illustrates a skills-as-agents creation surface.

nodes.label: Backend Platforms
nodes.short_description: Self-hostable backend platforms for building, deploying, and serving LLM-powered agents and apps.
nodes.long_description: Full-stack platforms — usually open source, often with a UI — that provide workflow, RAG, tool, and model plumbing behind an API. Contrast with libraries (frameworks) or managed SaaS: these are deployable services developers run to host agent applications.
nodes.examples_narrative: Dify is a representative self-hosted agent/LLM app backend platform.

nodes.label: CLIs/Toolkits
nodes.short_description: Command-line agents and developer toolkits for running agents from the terminal.
nodes.long_description: CLI-first agent surfaces — coding agents, general assistants, and SDK-style toolkits invoked from a shell. Sits at the developer-interface layer of the stack, distinct from web frontends or backend services.

nodes.label: Vendor
nodes.short_description: Vendor-published official CLI agents shipped by model providers as first-party terminal tools.
nodes.long_description: Official command-line agents released by the companies that train or host the underlying models. Distinguished from community CLIs by being the canonical vendor distribution.
nodes.examples_narrative: Qwen Code is Alibaba's official terminal coding agent for the Qwen models.

nodes.label: Computer Use
nodes.short_description: Agents and frameworks that operate a computer the way a human would (mouse, keyboard, screen).

nodes.label: Development
nodes.short_description: Software-engineering agents that read, write, and modify code to complete development tasks.
nodes.long_description: Agents purpose-built for coding work — repo navigation, patch generation, test running, PR authoring. Distinct from general assistants by their tight integration with code, shells, and dev-loop feedback.
nodes.examples_narrative: OpenHands is a canonical autonomous software-engineering agent.

nodes.label: Domain Specific
nodes.short_description: Agents specialised for a particular vertical or professional domain rather than general use.
nodes.long_description: Parent slot for agents whose value comes from domain expertise — biotech, legal, finance, and similar. Children split by the specific field; membership implies the agent is meaningfully tailored to that domain's data, tools, or workflows.

nodes.label: Biotech
nodes.short_description: Agents specialised for biotech, bioinformatics, and life-sciences research workflows.
nodes.long_description: Agents built to assist with biology and biotech tasks such as literature mining, sequence analysis, lab protocol generation, and experiment planning. They typically wrap domain tools and datasets behind an LLM-driven interface.

nodes.label: Frameworks
nodes.short_description: General-purpose frameworks for building agents and agentic applications.

nodes.label: Open Source
nodes.short_description: Open-source general-purpose agent frameworks under permissive or copyleft licences.
nodes.long_description: Freely available frameworks for building agents and agentic applications, distinguished from proprietary or hosted alternatives by their open codebases. They provide core loops, tool integration, and orchestration primitives developers can run and modify locally.

nodes.label: Frontends
nodes.short_description: User-facing interfaces — web, desktop, mobile, terminal — for interacting with agents.

nodes.label: Harnesses
nodes.short_description: Test harnesses and runner frameworks that host and exercise agents.

nodes.label: CLIs
nodes.short_description: Command-line harnesses that host and run agents in a terminal environment.
nodes.long_description: CLI-shaped runners that execute agents interactively or non-interactively from the shell. They sit at the execution layer of the stack, wrapping a model plus tools behind a terminal UX, and split into vendor-built and third-party implementations.

nodes.label: Third Party
nodes.short_description: Third-party CLI harnesses not built by the underlying model vendor.
nodes.long_description: Community or independent CLI runners that host agents against one or more model providers. They offer alternative terminal UXs and feature sets distinct from vendor-shipped CLIs.

nodes.label: Vendor
nodes.short_description: Vendor-shipped CLI harnesses built by the model provider themselves.
nodes.long_description: Official terminal agent runners published by the company behind the underlying model, such as Google's Gemini CLI or Anthropic's Claude Code. They provide a first-party execution surface tightly coupled to the vendor's models and APIs.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.short_description: Memory systems for agents — short-term, long-term, episodic, file-backed, graph-backed.

nodes.label: Graph Builders
nodes.short_description: Memory systems that construct and maintain knowledge graphs from agent interactions.
nodes.long_description: Tools that extract entities and relations from conversations, documents, or events and persist them as a graph used for long-term agent memory. They sit between raw logs and retrieval, turning unstructured history into structured, queryable memory.

nodes.label: Purpose Specific
nodes.short_description: Agents built for a specific operational purpose that does not map to a domain vertical.
nodes.long_description: A slot for agents defined by the task they perform (e.g. penetration testing, QA, triage) rather than by industry or framework. Distinguished from Domain Specific, which groups by vertical, and from Frameworks, which group by building blocks.

nodes.label: Penetration Testing
nodes.short_description: Agents that autonomously perform offensive security and penetration testing tasks.
nodes.long_description: Security-focused agents that enumerate, probe, and exploit systems in a controlled way, chaining recon and exploitation tools under LLM direction. They sit alongside traditional pentesting frameworks as an autonomous execution layer.

nodes.label: Team Formation
nodes.short_description: Tools that assemble and configure multi-agent teams from roles or task descriptions.
nodes.long_description: Systems whose job is to decide which agents to spawn, with what roles, tools, and prompts, to tackle a given objective. They sit upstream of multi-agent runtimes, producing the team that another layer then executes.

nodes.label: Teams
nodes.short_description: Preconfigured multi-agent teams targeting a specific workflow or domain.
nodes.long_description: Ready-made collections of cooperating agents wired together for a particular use case, such as data science or content production. Distinct from Team Formation (which builds teams) and from Frameworks (which provide the primitives).

nodes.label: Vision
nodes.short_description: Agents whose primary modality is visual perception of images or video.
nodes.long_description: Agents built around vision models that analyse, describe, or act on image and video inputs, often as part of a broader multimodal loop. They sit alongside text and computer-use agents as a distinct perceptual slot in the stack.

nodes.label: AI Browsers
nodes.short_description: Browsers built or adapted specifically for AI agent use, including headless variants optimised for programmatic navigation.

nodes.label: Headless
nodes.short_description: Headless browsers optimised for programmatic navigation by AI agents.
nodes.long_description: Browser engines without a visible UI, tuned for automated page loading, DOM access, and scraping under agent control. They sit below agent frameworks as the execution substrate for web interaction, competing with general-purpose headless tools repurposed for AI use.

nodes.label: Automation
nodes.short_description: General-purpose automation tools and frameworks, including GUI-driven and script-driven flows.

nodes.label: Open Source
nodes.short_description: Open-source automation platforms and frameworks for building agentic workflows.
nodes.long_description: Self-hostable automation tools that power agent-driven workflows, spanning GUI-first builders and script-based engines. Sits alongside proprietary SaaS automation platforms but distinguished by open licensing and self-hosting.

nodes.label: GUI Based
nodes.short_description: Open-source visual/no-code builders for composing automation and agent workflows.
nodes.long_description: Drag-and-drop canvas tools where flows, triggers and agent steps are wired visually rather than in code. Typically self-hosted, targeting makers who want n8n/Zapier-style UX with open licensing.

nodes.label: Backend Querying
nodes.short_description: Tools that let agents query backend systems, databases and APIs.

nodes.label: Benchmarks
nodes.short_description: Standardised benchmarks for evaluating agent and model capabilities.

nodes.label: Browser Automation
nodes.short_description: Libraries and services for driving real browsers under agent control.

nodes.label: Browser Tooling
nodes.short_description: Utilities and SDKs that sit alongside browsers to support agentic workflows.

nodes.label: Computer Use
nodes.short_description: Agents and frameworks that operate a computer the way a human would (mouse, keyboard, screen).

nodes.label: Platform Specific
nodes.short_description: Computer-use agents targeted at a specific operating system rather than cross-platform.
nodes.long_description: Groups computer-use implementations that exploit OS-native APIs, accessibility layers or UI conventions of one platform. Sits beside CLI and vision-based UI approaches as a delivery form factor for computer-use agents.

nodes.label: macOS
nodes.short_description: Computer-use agents built specifically for macOS desktops.
nodes.long_description: Agents that drive macOS via AppleScript, Accessibility APIs, or native automation hooks to control apps and the desktop. Distinct from generic vision-based computer-use by leaning on Mac-specific primitives.

nodes.label: Context
nodes.short_description: Tools for building, optimising, managing and visualising the context window supplied to LLMs.

nodes.label: Context Optimisation
nodes.short_description: Tools that shrink, prioritise or restructure context before it reaches the model.
nodes.long_description: Middleware focused on making the context window more efficient — pruning, compressing, reordering or mode-switching content for an LLM call. Sits between context assembly and the model itself.

nodes.label: Engines
nodes.short_description: Runtime engines that assemble and serve context to agents on demand.
nodes.long_description: Backends that fetch, merge and format context from multiple sources into a prompt-ready payload. The server-side counterpart to context optimisation and visualisation tools.

nodes.label: Frontend Tools
nodes.short_description: Client-side tools for capturing or selecting context from a running UI.
nodes.long_description: Browser/app-side utilities that let users grab DOM fragments, components or page state and feed them into an agent. Typically a bridge between a live frontend and a context engine.

nodes.label: Tools
nodes.short_description: Utility tools, SDKs and toolkits for building, debugging and extending agentic systems.

nodes.label: Context Managers
nodes.short_description: Utilities that organise, persist and version the context a developer feeds into coding agents.
nodes.long_description: Tooling for curating dotfile-style context bundles, rules and memory snippets used by AI coding assistants. Acts as a local source of truth for what gets injected into each session.

nodes.label: Visualisation
nodes.short_description: Tools for inspecting and visualising what is inside an LLM context window.
nodes.long_description: Debug and introspection utilities that render tokens, messages and attached artefacts so developers can see exactly what the model received. Complements context optimisation by exposing waste and overlap.

nodes.label: Conversational Control
nodes.short_description: Systems that steer or constrain multi-turn conversations with agents.

nodes.label: Data Extraction and Structuring
nodes.short_description: Tools that convert unstructured data into structured form for agents to consume.

nodes.label: Data Sources
nodes.short_description: Curated data feeds and providers that agents can draw on.

nodes.label: Financial
nodes.short_description: Financial and markets data feeds exposed as tools or sources for agents.
nodes.long_description: Providers of equities, crypto, macro and company fundamentals data packaged for programmatic agent consumption. Sits in the data layer beneath research and trading agents.

nodes.label: Databases
nodes.short_description: Database systems used in agentic stacks, including purpose-built context and vector stores.

nodes.label: Context Databases
nodes.short_description: Databases specifically designed for storing and retrieving agent/LLM context.

nodes.label: Deployment Platforms
nodes.short_description: Platforms for deploying and hosting agents and agentic services.

nodes.label: Serverless
nodes.short_description: Serverless runtimes for hosting agents without managing long-lived infrastructure.
nodes.long_description: Platforms that run agent workflows on-demand with managed scaling, state and scheduling. Occupies the same slot as containerised agent hosts but with a function-style execution model.

nodes.label: Dev Tools
nodes.short_description: Developer tooling that uses agents or supports agent development workflows.

nodes.label: Repo Refactoring
nodes.short_description: Agent-assisted tooling for restructuring and refactoring entire repositories.
nodes.long_description: Tools that apply large-scale, agent-driven transformations across a codebase — renames, moves, config migrations, architectural shifts. Distinct from IDE-level assistants by operating at repo scope.

nodes.label: Development Platforms
nodes.short_description: End-to-end platforms for building agentic applications.

nodes.label: Embedding Models
nodes.short_description: Models used to produce embeddings for retrieval and RAG pipelines.

nodes.label: Embedding Pipeline Orchestrators
nodes.short_description: Orchestration tools that manage embedding generation and ingestion pipelines.

nodes.label: Engineering Platforms
nodes.short_description: Platforms targeted at engineering teams building with LLMs and agents.

nodes.label: Environments
nodes.short_description: Sandboxed environments — OS, simulated worlds, virtual machines — in which agents run.

nodes.label: OS
nodes.short_description: Agent-native operating systems providing a full OS abstraction for autonomous agents to run within.
nodes.long_description: OS-level environments purpose-built as runtimes for agents, exposing processes, filesystem, and system calls as primitives the agent can control. Sits below frameworks and harnesses as the host substrate.

nodes.label: Remote Desktops
nodes.short_description: Hosted remote desktop / VM services providing agents with a persistent GUI environment to operate.

nodes.label: Evals and Experiment Tracking
nodes.short_description: Frameworks, harnesses and platforms for evaluating agents and tracking experiments.

nodes.label: Frameworks
nodes.short_description: General-purpose frameworks for building agents and agentic applications.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.short_description: Logging, tracing and observability specifically for agent and LLM systems.

nodes.label: Feedback Formatting
nodes.short_description: Tools that shape feedback signals (tool output, user input) into forms agents can act on.

nodes.label: Financial Tools
nodes.short_description: Agent-oriented tools targeted at financial workflows.

nodes.label: Formatting
nodes.short_description: Token-efficient serialisation and data-formatting schemes for passing structured data to and from LLMs.

nodes.label: Foundations
nodes.short_description: Industry foundations, consortia and non-profits shaping the agentic AI ecosystem.

nodes.label: Frameworks
nodes.short_description: General-purpose frameworks for building agents and agentic applications.

nodes.label: .NET
nodes.short_description: .NET/C# agent frameworks for building agents in the Microsoft ecosystem.
nodes.long_description: Agent-building frameworks targeting the .NET runtime and C# developers. Provides idiomatic APIs, DI, and tooling for embedding LLM agents into .NET applications and services.

nodes.label: Computer Use
nodes.short_description: Agents and frameworks that operate a computer the way a human would (mouse, keyboard, screen).

nodes.label: Multiagent Orchestration
nodes.short_description: Frameworks for coordinating multiple cooperating agents with roles, messaging, and task delegation.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks whose primary abstraction is a team of agents: role assignment, inter-agent messaging, shared state, and task routing. Used to compose workflows where specialised agents collaborate rather than a single monolithic agent.

nodes.label: Multimodal
nodes.short_description: Agent frameworks with first-class support for non-text modalities such as audio, image, and video.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks designed around multimodal input and output — video, image, audio streams — rather than text-only chat. Handles media I/O plumbing, streaming, and model routing for agents that perceive or produce rich media.

nodes.label: Optimisation
nodes.short_description: Frameworks focused on optimising agent execution — latency, cost, throughput, parallelism.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks whose differentiator is execution efficiency rather than abstractions for authoring agents. Covers parallel dispatch, batching, and cost/latency tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Parallelisation
nodes.short_description: Frameworks that parallelise agent or subtask execution across workers for throughput.
nodes.long_description: Tools that fan out agent work — steps, tool calls, or whole agent runs — across parallel workers. Targets throughput-bound workloads where serial agent loops are the bottleneck.

nodes.label: Social Simulations
nodes.short_description: Frameworks for simulating populations of agents modelling social, economic, or behavioural dynamics.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks for agent-based social simulation: many LLM-backed personas interacting under rules to study emergent group behaviour. Used for research, game-like worlds, and counterfactual studies rather than task execution.

nodes.label: Stateful Design
nodes.short_description: Frameworks built around persistent, long-lived agent state and memory as the core abstraction.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks where durable agent state — memory, identity, long-running context — is the central primitive rather than a bolt-on. Agents persist across sessions and evolve their internal state over time.

nodes.label: Voice
nodes.short_description: Voice-first agents and voice interaction frameworks.

nodes.label: Frontends
nodes.short_description: User-facing interfaces — web, desktop, mobile, terminal — for interacting with agents.

nodes.label: Android
nodes.short_description: Native Android apps that serve as frontends for interacting with agents on mobile.
nodes.long_description: Android-specific client applications for chatting with and operating agents from a phone. Handles mobile UX, background behaviour, and on-device integrations distinct from web or desktop clients.

nodes.label: Chat UIs
nodes.short_description: Web or desktop chat interfaces purpose-built as frontends for agents rather than plain LLMs.
nodes.long_description: Chat-style user interfaces tailored for agent interaction — tool call rendering, step traces, artefact display — rather than generic LLM chat. Typically web-based and framework-agnostic on the backend.

nodes.label: GUIs and CLIs
nodes.short_description: Dual GUI-and-CLI agent clients offering both graphical and command-line interaction modes.
nodes.long_description: Agent frontends that ship as both a graphical desktop app and a command-line tool, sharing a backend. Lets users pick interaction style per task without switching agents.

nodes.label: TUIs and CLIs
nodes.short_description: Terminal user interfaces and command-line clients for interacting with agents.
nodes.long_description: Text-mode frontends — TUIs and CLIs — for driving agents from the terminal. Favoured by developers for scriptability, low overhead, and integration into shell workflows.

nodes.label: Gateways
nodes.short_description: Gateway services that sit between agents and upstream services (LLMs, tools, data).

nodes.label: Context Compression
nodes.short_description: Gateways that compress or distill context before it reaches the model.
nodes.long_description: Proxy-layer services that sit between agents and LLMs to shrink prompts, summarize history, or prune tool output, reducing token spend and latency. Distinct from memory systems in that they operate inline on the request path rather than storing state.

nodes.label: Geopolitical Simulation
nodes.short_description: Simulations focused specifically on geopolitical scenarios and dynamics.

nodes.label: Harnesses
nodes.short_description: Execution-layer scaffolding that wraps agents for real runs — sub-agent dispatch, long-horizon coherence, checkpointing, platform-specific runtimes.
nodes.long_description: Harnesses sit between frameworks (which define agent shape) and runtimes (which provide the sandbox). They're the execution-layer utilities that make long-running, multi-step agent runs actually work in practice: spawning isolated sub-agent contexts, resuming from checkpoints, recovering from tool failures, keeping the main thread coherent over time, and adapting to the host platform.

nodes.label: Deep Research
nodes.short_description: Harnesses for multi-step research workflows with planning, tool use, and synthesis.

nodes.label: LLMs
nodes.short_description: Language models, including those trained or tuned specifically for agentic use cases.

nodes.label: Agent Specific
nodes.short_description: LLMs trained or tuned specifically for agentic and multi-agent workloads.
nodes.long_description: Models whose training objective, post-training, or native harness targets tool use, planning, or multi-agent coordination rather than general chat. Sits at the model layer of the stack and is chosen when vanilla instruction-tuned models fall short on agent benchmarks.

nodes.label: By Use Case
nodes.short_description: LLMs grouped by the agentic use case they are optimized for.
nodes.long_description: Organizes models by the downstream agentic task they target — computer use, coding, browsing, and so on — rather than by vendor or size. A parent bucket whose children are the specific use-case slots.

nodes.label: Computer Use
nodes.short_description: Agents and frameworks that operate a computer the way a human would (mouse, keyboard, screen).

nodes.label: Loops
nodes.short_description: Agent loop implementations — the core think/act/observe cycle.

nodes.label: Marketplaces
nodes.short_description: Marketplaces and stores for discovering, listing, or transacting agents and agent-based services.

nodes.label: MCP
nodes.short_description: Projects in the Model Context Protocol ecosystem — servers, gateways, aggregators, clients.

nodes.label: Aggregation
nodes.short_description: Aggregators that bundle many MCP servers behind a single endpoint.
nodes.long_description: Meta-MCP services that expose dozens or hundreds of upstream MCP servers through one unified connection, handling discovery, auth, and routing. Reduces per-client configuration burden when an agent needs access to a broad tool surface.

nodes.label: Gateways
nodes.short_description: Gateway services that sit between agents and upstream services (LLMs, tools, data).

nodes.label: Third Party
nodes.short_description: Community-built MCP servers wrapping third-party SaaS and APIs.
nodes.long_description: Unofficial MCP server implementations that expose external products and APIs (Airbnb, Spotify, etc.) as MCP tools, built outside the vendor. The catch-all slot for community MCP coverage of commercial services.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.short_description: Memory systems for agents — short-term, long-term, episodic, file-backed, graph-backed.

nodes.label: File
nodes.short_description: File-backed memory stores for agents.
nodes.long_description: Memory backends that persist agent state to files — flat, encoded, or media-container formats — rather than databases or vector stores. Useful for portable, inspectable, or offline agent memory.

nodes.label: Regressive
nodes.short_description: Memory that refines itself by regressing over past interactions.
nodes.long_description: Memory systems that iteratively re-process prior context to extract, correct, or compress learned signal, rather than just appending new entries. Targets long-running agents whose context quality must improve over time.

nodes.label: Skills
nodes.short_description: Agent skills ecosystems — registries, bundles, standards, guides and package libraries.

nodes.label: Multiagent
nodes.short_description: Systems built around multiple cooperating or competing agents.

nodes.label: Large Scale Experiments
nodes.short_description: Frameworks for running simulations with thousands of agents.
nodes.long_description: Engines built to scale multi-agent populations into the thousands or millions for social, economic, or emergent-behavior experiments. Distinct from production orchestration — the goal is research and simulation, not task delivery.

nodes.label: Swarm Prediction
nodes.short_description: Swarms of agents aggregated to produce forecasts or predictions.
nodes.long_description: Multi-agent systems where many independent agents vote, bid, or debate to generate a collective prediction, mimicking prediction-market or wisdom-of-crowds dynamics. The output is a forecast signal rather than a completed task.

nodes.label: Networks
nodes.short_description: Agent networks and directories — social/professional platforms where agents and their creators are listed and interconnected.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.short_description: Logging, tracing and observability specifically for agent and LLM systems.

nodes.label: On Device Agents
nodes.short_description: Agents designed to run on-device, including phones, SBCs and edge hardware.

nodes.label: SBCs
nodes.short_description: Agent runtimes targeting single-board computers and edge hardware.
nodes.long_description: Agent stacks built for Raspberry Pi class boards and similar SBCs, optimized for constrained CPU, RAM, and power budgets. Sibling to phone-specific runtimes in the on-device layer.

nodes.label: Operations
nodes.short_description: Operational concerns for running agents in production — cost, monitoring, reliability.

nodes.label: Cost Monitoring
nodes.short_description: Tools that track token spend and cost for production agents.
nodes.long_description: Observability systems focused on financial metrics — per-run, per-agent, and per-tool cost attribution for LLM and tool calls. Distinct from general tracing or evals; the primary surface is a spend dashboard.

nodes.label: Optimisation Platform
nodes.short_description: Platforms focused on optimising agent/LLM pipelines.

nodes.label: Orchestration
nodes.short_description: Orchestration engines for coordinating agents, tools and workflows.

nodes.label: Platform-Specific
nodes.short_description: Orchestration frameworks tied to a specific cloud or vendor platform.
nodes.long_description: Agent orchestration engines that are coupled to one provider's runtime, SDKs, or services rather than being platform-neutral. Chosen when a team has already committed to that platform's ecosystem.

nodes.label: Personal AI
nodes.short_description: Personal AI assistants and related projects.

nodes.label: Personification
nodes.short_description: Tools for giving agents distinct personas, voices and identities.

nodes.label: Pipelines
nodes.short_description: Reusable pipelines for common agentic workloads.

nodes.label: Platforms
nodes.short_description: General platforms that host or enable agentic applications.

nodes.label: Proxy
nodes.short_description: Proxy services that sit between clients and LLM/agent backends.

nodes.label: Gateways
nodes.short_description: Gateway services that sit between agents and upstream services (LLMs, tools, data).

nodes.label: RAG
nodes.short_description: Retrieval-augmented generation engines, frameworks and components.

nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning
nodes.short_description: RL frameworks and environments used to train agentic behaviour.

nodes.label: Routers
nodes.short_description: Routing layers that select between models, tools or agents based on cost, capability or intent.

nodes.label: Runtimes
nodes.short_description: Execution runtimes — sandboxes, data layers, kernels — that host running agents.

nodes.label: Policy Engines
nodes.short_description: Runtime policy engines that evaluate and enforce rules on agent actions.

nodes.label: SaaS Builders
nodes.short_description: Hosted SaaS platforms for building agents and agentic workflows, spanning no-code, visual, and code-defined paradigms.

nodes.label: No Code
nodes.short_description: Builders targeted at non-programmers, typically form- and config-driven with minimal visual wiring.

nodes.label: Visual Programming
nodes.short_description: Builders where flows are expressed as node-and-edge graphs or canvas-based visual programs.

nodes.label: Workflow Focused
nodes.short_description: Visual builders oriented around structured, multi-step workflows rather than free-form agents.

nodes.label: Code-Defined
nodes.short_description: Hosted builders where agents and workflows are primarily defined in code (SDK, DSL, or config-as-code).

nodes.label: Security
nodes.short_description: Security tooling for agents: sandboxing, red teaming, defensive automation.

nodes.label: Automation
nodes.short_description: General-purpose automation tools and frameworks, including GUI-driven and script-driven flows.

nodes.label: Control Planes
nodes.short_description: Centralised control planes for governing, auditing, and enforcing policy across agent fleets.

nodes.label: Skills
nodes.short_description: Agent skills ecosystems — registries, bundles, standards, guides and package libraries.

nodes.label: Standards
nodes.short_description: Proposed and emerging standards across the agentic ecosystem.

nodes.label: Societal Simulations
nodes.short_description: Large-scale simulations of societies and human behaviour using agent populations.

nodes.label: Software Extensions
nodes.short_description: Extensions and plugins that add agentic capabilities to existing software.

nodes.label: Standards
nodes.short_description: Proposed and emerging standards across the agentic ecosystem.

nodes.label: Emerging (Ecosystem-Tied)
nodes.short_description: Emerging standards whose adoption is tied to a specific host ecosystem (e.g. a browser engine or platform vendor) rather than being fully vendor-neutral.

nodes.label: Ratified
nodes.short_description: Standards with a published specification and active governance.

nodes.label: Starter Kits
nodes.short_description: Opinionated starter kits and templates for kicking off agentic projects.

nodes.label: Synthesised Perspectives
nodes.short_description: Tools that synthesise multiple viewpoints or agent outputs into a unified perspective.

nodes.label: Task Specific
nodes.short_description: Agents and tools built for a single specific task rather than general use.

nodes.label: Trainers
nodes.short_description: Training frameworks and tools for agent models.

nodes.label: Uncategorized
nodes.short_description: Holding area for projects awaiting classification. Entries here are a todo list, not a destination.

nodes.label: Upserting Tools
nodes.short_description: Tools that manage upserting data into vector stores and retrieval indexes.

nodes.label: UX
nodes.short_description: User-experience patterns and components specifically for agent systems.

nodes.label: Human in the Loop
nodes.short_description: Interfaces and workflows for inserting humans into agent execution — approvals, task handoff, review queues.

nodes.label: Vector Databases
nodes.short_description: Vector databases used for embedding storage and similarity search.

nodes.label: Voice
nodes.short_description: Voice-first agents and voice interaction frameworks.

nodes.label: Workflow Definition
nodes.short_description: Tools and DSLs for authoring agent workflows as first-class, portable artifacts.

nodes.label: Workspaces
nodes.short_description: Shared workspaces where agents and humans (or multiple agents) collaborate.

nodes.label: World Generation
nodes.short_description: Tools and models for generating simulated worlds for agents to operate in.

nodes.label: Android
nodes.short_description: Android-specific agent runtimes and device-level automation.

nodes.label: CLI
nodes.short_description: CLI-based computer-use agents and tools.

nodes.label: Ingestion
nodes.short_description: Pipelines that ingest and process documents into retrieval systems.

nodes.label: Debugging
nodes.short_description: Debugging and introspection tools for agent development.

nodes.label: IDE Integrations
nodes.short_description: IDE extensions and plugins for agent-assisted development.

nodes.label: Integrations
nodes.short_description: Agent-to-SaaS connector platforms: Composio-style libraries exposing third-party APIs as agent tools.

nodes.label: Semantic Search
nodes.short_description: Semantic search tools for agents operating over codebases and local content.

nodes.label: Vision UI
nodes.short_description: Vision-based UI understanding models for computer-use agents.

nodes.label: MCP
nodes.short_description: MCP servers for world and scene generation.

nodes.label: Prompt Management
nodes.short_description: Versioning, registries, and lifecycle management for prompts as first-class artifacts.

nodes.label: Structured Output
nodes.short_description: Libraries that constrain LLM outputs to typed schemas, JSON, or formal grammars.

nodes.label: Semantic Caching
nodes.short_description: Cache layers that serve semantically equivalent LLM responses to cut cost and latency.

nodes.label: Inference Servers
nodes.short_description: LLM inference servers and runtimes (vLLM, TGI, llama.cpp, SGLang) that agents call into.

nodes.label: Synthetic Data
nodes.short_description: Tools for generating synthetic training and evaluation data for agents.

nodes.label: Annotation
nodes.short_description: Human labeling, RLHF, and feedback-collection platforms for agent training data.

nodes.label: Identity and Auth
nodes.short_description: Agent identity, credential vaulting, and delegated authentication for tool access.

nodes.label: PII and Redaction
nodes.short_description: PII detection, content sanitization, and safety filtering for agent I/O.

nodes.label: Agent Analytics
nodes.short_description: Product analytics for agent behaviour: funnels, retention, conversation quality, task success.

nodes.label: Guardrails
nodes.short_description: Guardrails and pre-execution safety checks that gate what running agents can do.

nodes.label: Desktop GUIs
nodes.short_description: Desktop graphical interfaces for interacting with agents.

nodes.label: Sub-agent Dispatch
nodes.short_description: Libraries and runtimes that spawn isolated sub-agent contexts to offload work and preserve main-thread coherence.

nodes.label: Checkpointing
nodes.short_description: Save and restore agent execution state across interruptions, crashes, or session boundaries.

nodes.label: Task Coherence
nodes.short_description: Progress trackers, self-reflection loops, and plan-revision utilities that keep long-running agent tasks on course.

nodes.label: Retry and Recovery
nodes.short_description: Failure handling, automatic rerun on tool errors, and partial-result recovery for agent loops.

nodes.label: Context Compression
nodes.short_description: Harness-level summarisation and pruning that rolls the context window during a run, distinct from gateway-level compression in the request path.

nodes.label: Platform Specific
nodes.short_description: Harnesses that target a specific host OS or environment for native integration.

nodes.label: macOS
nodes.short_description: Harnesses that run agents natively on macOS.
